body_txt: Social networking services, also called social media, have revolutionized the ways people communicate, form relationships, learn news and trends, get famous and/or waste hours every day. It's only natural that they've started showing up in works of fiction. But oftentimes, the author will choose to invent a fictional social network rather than depict a real one, either due to fear of getting sued, or just to give it the exact set of features that fit the story. Generally a Bland-Name Product of a popular real platform such as Facebook , Twitter , Instagram , YouTube or TikTok — though sometimes a more obscure platform will be parodied instead (such as MySpace ) or the author will invent a unique website concept (often combining features of several real ones). Sometimes the platform is limited, such as a network only for the faculty and students of a particular school. This permits the authors more freedom when, for instance, a suspect pool can be only a few hundred users rather than a few million, or when the protagonist finds himself the guy in charge of the whole thing. May be used to impart a Social Media Is Bad aesop. Compare The Alternet (a whole fictional internet, not just one website/app), Shallow News Site Satire .
